speaker: interviewer
text: Survivor is Martin Perlmutter. This is roll number 2.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans had established a labor camp in Sasow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And there was a quarry. And they were breaking up stones for roads work. But I was sent, as I mentioned, to carry water up the hill, which was about a mile up the hill. And a German soldier was behind us with a gun. There were six people carrying water for them to take a bath or whatever their needs were because there was no running water anywheres in my-- in the area-- no electricity. They had electricity in there with a radio station of their own. But in town, there was no electricity. So we carried those pails of water up the hill constantly a whole day. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The Hauptsturmfuhrer Warzog was in charge of the labor camp in Sasow at that time. One day, I went to Pobocz with my girlfriend, Chana Felig. And I purchased a, a chicken, which I hid behind my shirt. And as I was walking, I saw Hauptsturmfuhrer Warzog on a horse, riding. And next to him was a policeman-- a kapo from-- a Yiddish kapo from the lager by the name of [NON-ENGLISH]. And as I was hiding off the road, the chicken flew out from under my shirt. And the, the Warzog's horse jumped. He pulled his gun and was going to shoot me and my girlfriend. And [NON-ENGLISH] persuaded him to let me explain what's going on. Well, he ordered me to come to camp to Lager Sasow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I refused to go. I didn't show up. Some days later, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he asked for me in, in the Judenrat. And he was told that I did not show up. At that time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he made a Aktion and a [NON-ENGLISH] and collected some dozen of Jews and brought them to Sasow to camp. And the Judenrat made sure that I show up there in, in camp, in lager. He asked for me, and made-- I saw with my own eyes that he shot all those Jews, all those people in front of us. And my girlfriend's father, Anszel Felig, and myself had to bury those people. It was an awful experience. It was a horrible experience. One of them that I recall vividly, he had a man there on crutches with one leg. He made him jump. He had him jump until he shot him dead. And I had to cover, to, to bury those bodies. I don't know whether he chose me because I refused to show up in, in lager. But if it wouldn't have been for me, it would have been somebody else to bury these people. But it made an awful impression on me. I cried for days. I was in, in bad psychological shape at the time. On the way back to Zloczow, Warzog stopped at Judenrat and told them to ship me out to Lackie Wielkie. Lackie Wielkie was already a larger camp, an established camp. It was much more difficult there than where-- than in Sasow. Over there, we slept on planks, wooden planks without anything, one above the other. And sometimes, we woke up in the morning, and my next-- the man next to me, sleeping next to me was dead. And I was there for quite a few months. And I worked on the quarries. And I worked on shoveling snow from Lackie Wielkie to Zloczow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the food like there? Did they feed you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were, we were getting some food. I don't recall how much of what. It was also possible for my girlfriend to come out to that camp and finding me on the road shoveling snow and giving me something to eat or whatever. My brother-in-law, who was also in the Judenrat, made arrangements some months later to transfer me to the [NON-ENGLISH], Zloczow. That was an improvement because we had already a place where to sleep-- a little more decent place to sleep. The verkstaden, they were called-- they had artisans from all professions-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shoemakers, furriers, clothiers. They were making all kinds of things for Sturmfuhrer Warzog at the time and for his friends and for his family in Germany. My teacher was there painting paintings-- making paintings for, for Warzog, which he also distributed as gifts and so forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were living at home still at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that-- no, I was in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your family was still at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: My family was transferred from Sasow to the ghetto Zloczow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When would that be?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would be-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that was in the late summer of 1942. My father passed away 25 days in the month of Adar. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That night, I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] got out from [NON-ENGLISH] camp in Zloczow and smuggled myself into the ghetto Zloczow to say Kaddish after my father. That same night, a Yiddish, a Jewish policeman came running to the verkstaden where we are, we were and told us that the ghetto Zloczow is surrounded by German SS, and a liquidation is taking place. There was a great deal of shooting, a great deal of commotion. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the next day, around 11 o'clock, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was working in the verkstaden, selecting the clothing of the people that were killed in, in [NON-ENGLISH]. That's about 3 kilometers, 3 1/2 kilometers near Zloczow-- between Zloczow and Sasow.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the killing ground.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were the killing grounds. And around 11 o'clock that morning, I found the clothes of my brother-in-law, my mother, my sister, my brother, and their five-year-old child. Clothing-- I found them while I was selecting and working in that, in that position.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do I know exactly that it were their clothes? Because I was wearing the jacket of a suit, and my brother-in-law was wearing the slacks from the same suit. And I recognized it. So I took it out separately. And in it, I found documentation of my family. And I found a $20 gold piece sewn in in his belt, belt area. And as I was selecting those clothes and found many valuables-- many diamonds and money and everything that was brought out and was separated to give to the Germans, that $20 piece, I kept. So I know for a fact that my family [NON-ENGLISH] was 26 days in the month of Adar, on 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] I also recall vividly that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the next morning I walked out into the streets of Zloczow to see what's going on because we could hear a lot of shooting and a lot of noises. We were not that far away from the ghetto. And I saw the rabbi's wife, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] their daughter, their son [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on a open truck, being transferred, obviously, to [NON-ENGLISH], to the killing grounds.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] wife. And she was screaming to me, [NON-ENGLISH], tell the world what you just saw. Tell the world what's going on here. That was an experience I'll never forget. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] 13 days in the month of July, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the verkstaden were liquidated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Warzog got a promotion to Hauptsturmfuhrer. And he was given the job to run the camp in Lemberg. [PAUSES FOR 22 SECONDS] We were transferred on that day to Janowska camp in Lemberg. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'd like to tell you a little bit about the camp in Lemberg. It was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a place where Nazis came to get trained in brutality.

speaker: interviewer
text: This camp is the subject of the famous book

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a series of books about Janowska written by my friend, Mihail [NON-ENGLISH]. His name was Borohowicz, a poet from Poland who was with me in Janowska. And after the war, he wrote many books about Janowska. One of the books he named [NON-ENGLISH], Every morning at Appell, they used to have an orchestra from famous musicians from around Europe. And they were playing, playing music. And as the people were walking out from their barracks [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the music playing, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] many of the Jews that were not healthy enough were pushed into the sides between barbed wires and were shot. Others, for any kind of [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] things that was done that was out of order, like not putting out their hat in time or not taking it off on time-- they were hung. They put people, and they hung them on poles with their feet up, their heads down, and took them until sometimes two, three days until they expired. It was-- they had trained dogs to rip you apart. People were walking out to many different places to work-- into town of Lemberg and into other verkstaden-- places where his own people from Zloczow was brought. And they were still making boots and clothing. And my, my teacher and myself, we worked still on paintings. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] When you went to the latrine, you could see between the cracks in the wood. The killing grounds, they were called the [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH] was an open pit where the Nazis were killing the people. They brought them from around the communities around Janowska and around, around Lemberg. Anybody that was caught or [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] found in, in hiding or something, they were brought there, and they were killed there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: At the end of November [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] 1942, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they were-- they brought logs, wooden logs. And they lined them up against the, the wire, the electrified wires that were around the camp. There were soldiers with lights. What do you call them? The-- Search lights.

speaker: interviewee
text: Search lights. And the, the wires were electrified. It gave us an opportunity-- myself and eight other people, nine people. I dug a hole. Let me, let me try to explain the situation there. Right next to the Janowska camp, there was a railroad track. Trains were running nearby, and there was an embankment like this. And the wooden logs were lined up like this, so it was not difficult to go behind those wooden logs and, and dig a hole at the tip of this embankment. And we could not be seen from-- by the searchlight-- by the soldiers near the searchlight because it was covered, the area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I dug a hole there with another friend of mine. I dug a hole, and nine of us ran out to freedom on the other side of the camp. All of us disappeared. All of us separated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was left alone to start walking on the other side of the track and came to a cemetery nearby, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I lay down and slept the night over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: My teacher, Nachimowicz, and a young man by the name of Adam-- I don't recall his family name-- were caught the next day. And from what I heard later on, they were thrown and ripped apart by the dogs. The dogs ripped them apart. Of the nine, five are still alive. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] From there, I walked [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] back to my, again, to nearby my hometown in the village of Pobocz. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When I came to a Ukrainian lady that was friendly to my parents, she just made a sign of, of a cross when she saw me. And she told me that she'll permit me to sleep in the barn overnight, but I have to disappear the next morning because her son-in-law is a policeman. And she told me that there are Jews in the woods nearby, and I should try to contact them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got to go back, a little, a little bit back and tell you another incident that happened to me when we were still-- after the liquidation of the ghetto Zloczow. A group of us, of about 20 Jews, decided not to wait to be killed, and we decided to run away from camp, from labor camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We acquired some guns, and we ran into the nearby woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you get the guns from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were Jewish policemen. And they were people that were close to, to Warzog.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Hauptsturmfuhrer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And we-- and they helped us acquire some guns. We ran away. It was against my good judgment to go to the woods near Zloczow. And we had an, an incident. On the first day that we [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] came to the woods-- I came also with my girlfriend, Chana-- we found a Pole, a Christian, in the woods. And there was a debate between us whether we should get rid of him or let him live.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shoot him.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. And, you know, we were not aggressive enough, or some of us were not aggressive enough. And they let him go. And of course, he notified a police that we are in the woods. And within a few hours that same night, they surrounded the woods area. My girlfriend, Chana, was killed there, and most of them, most of the others were killed. They threw in hand grenades and all kinds of-- they didn't dare walk into the woods. My teacher and I were able to escape. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm trying to go back to the time between the liquidation of the ghetto in Zloczow and the transfer of the [NON-ENGLISH] camp in Zloczow to Janowska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: From there, my teacher and I, of course, went back to Pobocz, to the area of the woods that I worked carrying water to the German Wehrmacht radio station. And I knew the woods there quite well. I acquired a spade-- a shovel. And we made ourselves a very nice bunker. We got some food with us, and we got-- we dug a, a bunker in the woods. A few days after this, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a young lady by the name of Frydka Brenner was caught. And Hauptsturmfuhrer Warzog was notified. And he came to look for us in the woods. He came upon the area where we were hiding, and he broke up our bunker. And on the shovel, he put a note on. And he said, if you return, we'll-- I'll let you live. And if you don't, I'll pursue you until your death. I got sick with a high fever. And my teacher, Nachimowicz, was willing to take a chance. And I had no choice to return, but to return to Zloczow. There was a Jewish policeman that was close to Warzog. And he brought us into his home. When we came in, I was very sick. I couldn't even stand erect, which was a prerequisite. He told them-- he kept his word. He told him to take us back to, to the verkstaden, give us a bath, and he let us live. We went back to work. And as I mentioned before, on the 13th of July, the verkstaden was liquidated. He went to Janowska camp in Lemberg. And he was--

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1942. Well, now I find myself, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] after running away, as I told you, I came back to Pobocz and went to the [INAUDIBLE] into the other woods in the area. And I found a group of Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I hooked up with a man by the name of Scholder and Jakob Ries. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I made [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a hole in a barn, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and during the day, we were just walking around. But at nighttime, we went into that barn. We took out some straw-- some hay-- and we slept the nights through there. On that occasion, once, I drew a portrait of these two people, Benjamin Scholder [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is alive. Jakob Ries was caught one morning. He went to, to the village to retrieve some of the materials-- some of the goods that his father left with the local population. And they killed him, and they dismembered his body. After that, the winters came, and it was difficult to find a place where to sleep at night, although we walked most of the time in the woods in the snow till I was found by a man by the name of Milyg Zilber. Milyg Zilber was a friend of mine from Zloczow. He, himself, was a son of a very wealthy man in [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] He was lucky to be in a bunker that a family, Szarf, built. They were local people from the village, from, from [NON-ENGLISH] and from [NON-ENGLISH]. And they knew the area quite well. The woods, they know it very well. And they never went to, to a ghetto or to a camp. They went from their homes into the woods, and they build themself a beautiful bunker. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he was, he was able to persuade these people, the Szarf family, to take me in. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All I can tell you is when I got there, I had to be outside the bunker until they let me in because I was full of lice. So after they were able to clean me up--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they, how did they manage to get rid of all the lice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was kerosene. They washed me. And they took the clothes, and they boiled it. And then they-- after a few days, they were able to get rid of it. Now, well, I stayed in that bunker, and that was like being at the Waldorf Astoria. It was-- they had food there. They had a stove. Over 20 people survived in that bunker.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a large bunker, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a large bunker. And the whole family-- now the family of Szarf [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] three brothers, one sister, a mother with three children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were a few other people there, and I was one of them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My future wife, Dora, was in that bunker. She was the one that was able to go out to acquire food because she was a blonde, and she was dressed like a peasant. And the local people knew who she was. There was a question of hiding the footsteps in the snow. It was a ingenious idea of one of the Szarf brothers to use a sieve and walk backwards and with the sieve, put snow in the sieve and use it like you use for, for flour.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sprinkling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sprinkling then. So if you walked backwards, you couldn't-- you could not walk into the woods without showing footsteps. So he devised this ingenious thing that to sieve the snow so that they, they-- it will cover the footsteps.
